title: Critical Reasoning: Strengthen
prerequisites: spot the assumption connecting evidence to conclusion
learning_objectives: Find the conclusion, then choose the fact that makes it more likely to be true.
Prefer answers that confirm the argument's assumption or rule out an alternative cause.
Reject choices that are merely on-topic but do not affect the conclusion.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do -> we do -> you do

items.prompt: To STRENGTHEN a causal claim, what is one powerful move?
items.answer: Rule out an alternative cause, or show the cause-effect holds in a controlled comparison.

items.prompt: Does a choice that repeats the conclusion strengthen it?
items.answer: No - restating is not new support.

items.prompt: Strengthen or not: evidence that a confounding factor was absent.

retrieval_starter.purpose: Recalls that strengthening usually means confirming the assumption or eliminating a rival cause.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: Remind them a strengthener need not prove the conclusion - just make it more likely.

questions.prompt: Name two ways to strengthen a causal argument.
questions.answer: Confirm the assumption; rule out an alternative cause.

questions.prompt: Why is 'the study had many participants' often a weak strengthener?
questions.answer: Sample size alone doesn't address the logical gap in the argument.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: You can find the assumption that props up an argument. A strengthen question rewards the choice that makes that assumption more secure - or slams the door on a competing explanation. Same gap, now you reinforce it instead of just naming it.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can pick the choice that most increases the likelihood the conclusion is true, usually by supporting the assumption or removing an alternative cause.
success_criteria: I can state the conclusion the choice must support.
I can tell a real strengthener from an on-topic distractor.
I can recognize 'rule out another cause' as a strengthening move.

opening_script.move: Do Now on screen; learners answer from memory, no notes.
Cold-call answers; name the argument's conclusion vs premises.
Bridge to today's question type; state what the stem is really asking.
Move to I do: model the reasoning on one worked item.

i_do.stem: A town noticed that crime fell in the year after it installed brighter streetlights, and concluded the lights caused the drop. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the conclusion?

options.A: In a neighboring town that installed no new lights that year, crime stayed the same.
options.B: The brighter streetlights were more energy-efficient than the old ones.
options.C: Residents said they felt safer after the lights were installed.
options.D: The town had wanted brighter lighting for several years.
options.E: Crime is generally higher in areas with more foot traffic.

i_do.explanation: A controlled comparison - a similar town with no new lights and no crime drop - rules out a region-wide trend, isolating the lights as the cause and strengthening the conclusion.
think_aloud_steps: Conclusion: the lights caused the crime drop (a causal claim).
To strengthen a cause, rule out that something else caused the drop.
A shows a comparable town without new lights had no drop - so the drop wasn't a general trend.
That isolates the lights as the cause; the others don't touch causation.
i_do.key_takeaway: For causal conclusions, the strongest strengthener usually eliminates an alternative cause via a controlled comparison.

we_do.stem: A company found that employees who use its new project-management software finish projects faster, and concluded the software makes teams more efficient. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens this conclusion?

options.A: When a team that had been slow was switched to the software, its completion times improved.
options.B: The software was developed by an award-winning design firm.
options.C: Employees say the software is easy to learn.
options.D: The software is used by many large companies.
options.E: Faster teams tend to have higher morale.

we_do.explanation: Showing the same previously-slow team sped up after adopting the software supports causation rather than the possibility that fast teams simply chose the software.
scaffold_hints: The claim is causal: software -> efficiency.
Watch for reverse causation: maybe efficient teams adopt the software.
Which choice shows the software CHANGING a team's speed?

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Yes - a before/after on the same team addresses reverse causation.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Popularity and design awards don't show the software caused the speed-up.

we_do.stem: A nutrition writer argues that a new breakfast cereal improves focus, citing that children who eat it score higher on morning attention tests. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

options.A: Before eating the cereal, those same children had scored no higher than other children on the tests.
options.B: The cereal is fortified with several vitamins.
options.C: The cereal is popular among parents.
options.D: Attention tests are administered by trained staff.
options.E: Children generally focus better after eating something.

we_do.explanation: If the high scorers were ordinary before the cereal, the cereal - not pre-existing ability - is the likely cause of the improvement, strengthening the claim.
scaffold_hints: Beware the alternative: maybe naturally focused kids eat this cereal.
A before/after within the same children rules that out.
Vitamins and popularity don't establish the focus effect.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Right - ruling out prior ability strengthens the causal link.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Choice E actually weakens by giving an alternative cause (any breakfast helps).

you_do.stem: A retailer concluded that its new loyalty program increased customer spending, because members spend more than non-members. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the conclusion?

options.A: Customers' spending rose after they joined the program, compared with their spending before joining.
options.B: The loyalty program was advertised on social media.
options.C: Members receive a discount on their birthdays.
options.D: The retailer has stores in many cities.
options.E: High spenders enjoy collecting reward points.

you_do.explanation: A within-customer before/after comparison shows the program changed behavior, rather than merely that big spenders join programs.

you_do.stem: A city argues that its new bike-share program reduced car traffic, noting traffic fell after the program launched. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

options.A: During the same period, a comparable city with no bike-share saw no decline in car traffic.
options.B: The bike-share stations are conveniently located.
options.D: The bikes are maintained by a private contractor.
options.E: Car traffic is usually lighter in warm weather.

you_do.explanation: A comparison city without bike-share and without a traffic drop rules out a general trend, isolating the program as the cause.

you_do.stem: Researchers claim a meditation app lowers stress, citing that users report less stress than non-users. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

options.A: Users who were randomly assigned to the app reported lower stress than those assigned no app.
options.B: The app includes guided sessions of varying lengths.
options.C: The app has millions of downloads.
options.D: Stress was measured with a standard questionnaire.
options.E: People who meditate often also exercise.

you_do.explanation: Random assignment rules out that already-calm people simply chose the app, giving strong support that the app itself lowers stress.

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
